title: Turn My To-Do List Into a Realistic Plan for Today
description: Transforms a messy task list into a clear day plan with priorities, time blocks, and what to defer.
prompt:
You are my productivity coach.
I will paste my full task list, deadlines, meetings, and energy level.
Create a realistic plan for today.

Please:
1. Identify the 3 most important outcomes.
2. Group the rest into do now, do later, delegate, or ignore.
3. Suggest a simple time-blocked schedule.
4. Warn me if I am trying to do too much.
5. End with a short, calming summary in plain language.

Here is my list and context:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when your day feels overloaded and you need clarity more than motivation.
exampleUse: Paste your scattered notes, deadlines, and appointments. The result gives you a tighter daily plan and a smaller set of priorities.

title: Run a Weekly Review and Plan the Next 7 Days
description: Helps you review what happened this week, spot loose ends, and set up a calmer next week.
prompt:
Help me run a weekly review.
I will share what happened this week, what is unfinished, what feels stressful, and what matters next week.

Please give me:
1. A short summary of wins, unfinished items, and hidden risks.
2. The top priorities for next week.
3. A suggested weekly plan with focus themes by day.
4. Anything I should stop doing or simplify.
5. One reflection question to carry into next week.

Here is my week:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this on Friday afternoon or Sunday evening when you want to reset before the next week starts.
exampleUse: Paste a few notes about your week and upcoming commitments. The response helps you wrap the week and start the next one with less mental clutter.

title: Convert Meeting Notes Into Clear Action Items
description: Takes rough meeting notes and turns them into decisions, owners, deadlines, and follow-up actions.
prompt:
I will paste raw meeting notes.
Turn them into a clean recap for a busy team.

Please:
1. Summarize the main decisions.
2. List action items with likely owners and deadlines.
3. Flag open questions or risks.
4. Rewrite unclear or vague notes into simple language.
5. End with a short follow-up message I can send to attendees.

Here are the notes:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this right after a meeting when you want to send a clear follow-up without rereading your notes for 20 minutes.
exampleUse: Paste a messy notes dump from Zoom or a notebook. The answer gives you a structured recap and a ready-to-send summary.

title: Write a Professional Follow-Up Email That Feels Human
description: Creates a polite, direct follow-up email without sounding stiff or passive aggressive.
prompt:
Help me write a professional follow-up email.
I will share the situation, what I need, my relationship to the person, and the tone I want.

Please write:
1. A subject line.
2. A short email that is clear, respectful, and confident.
3. One softer version and one more direct version.
4. A note explaining which version is best for this situation.

Situation:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you need a reminder email that protects the relationship while still moving things forward.
exampleUse: Describe the context, who you are writing to, and what you need back. The result gives you multiple email versions so you can match the moment.

title: Turn Rough Notes Into a Clear Message
description: Takes half-formed thoughts and shapes them into a clear message, post, or paragraph with better flow.
prompt:
I will paste rough notes, fragments, or bullet points.
Turn them into a clear, readable message.

Please:
1. Identify the main point.
2. Remove repetition and filler.
3. Rewrite it in plain, confident language.
4. Keep my original intent.
5. Offer a shorter version if I need something tighter.

My notes:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you know what you want to say but your thoughts still feel messy on the page.
exampleUse: Paste scattered bullets from your notes app. The response turns them into a cleaner email, paragraph, or post draft.

title: Rewrite This So It Sounds Natural and Human
description: Softens robotic writing and makes it sound clearer, warmer, and more natural without losing the point.
prompt:
Rewrite the text I share so it sounds more natural and human.

Please:
1. Keep the meaning the same.
2. Remove robotic or overly formal phrasing.
3. Make it easier to read.
4. Keep it concise.
5. Give me two options: one neutral and one warmer.

Text:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when your draft says the right thing but sounds too stiff, flat, or AI-written.
exampleUse: Paste a draft email, caption, or web paragraph. The answer gives you versions with a more natural tone.

title: Teach Me a Topic Like a Great Coach Would
description: Breaks down a topic step by step, using simple explanations, analogies, and checkpoints for understanding.
prompt:
Teach me this topic as if you are a great coach.
I want practical understanding, not jargon.

Please:
1. Explain the topic simply.
2. Use one everyday analogy.
3. Break it into key ideas in the right order.
4. Tell me the most common beginner mistakes.
5. End with 3 quick questions so I can test my understanding.

Topic:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when a concept feels intimidating and you want it explained in a calm, structured way.
exampleUse: Paste a topic like inflation, APIs, or probability. The reply explains it more like a tutor than a textbook.

title: Build a Study Plan From My Goal and Deadline
description: Creates a simple study roadmap from your goal, timeframe, available hours, and current skill level.
prompt:
Create a study plan for me.
I will share what I want to learn, my current level, how much time I have, and my deadline.

Please give me:
1. A realistic weekly study plan.
2. The order I should learn things.
3. What to practice each week.
4. A checkpoint system so I know if I am progressing.
5. Advice on how to avoid burnout or overload.

My goal and context:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you have a learning goal but do not know how to pace it realistically.
exampleUse: Describe your goal, timeline, and available study time. The result turns it into a clearer, lower-stress learning path.

title: Turn a Bug Report Into a Smart Debugging Plan
description: Takes a bug report and returns a structured debugging plan with likely causes and the fastest next checks.
prompt:
I will share a bug report, the observed behavior, expected behavior, and any clues.
Turn it into a practical debugging plan.

Please:
1. Restate the bug clearly.
2. List the most likely root causes in priority order.
3. Suggest the fastest checks or logs to add first.
4. Separate probable issues from speculative ones.
5. Recommend a fix strategy once the cause is confirmed.

Bug details:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you want to debug methodically instead of jumping between random guesses.
exampleUse: Paste a ticket description or your own notes from a broken feature. The reply gives you a cleaner sequence for troubleshooting.

title: Explain This Code Without Unnecessary Jargon
description: Explains unfamiliar code in plain language, including what it does, why it exists, and what to watch out for.
prompt:
Explain this code to me in plain language.

Please:
1. Tell me what it does overall.
2. Walk through the important parts in order.
3. Explain why each part matters.
4. Point out any risks, assumptions, or edge cases.
5. End with a simpler mental model I can remember.

Code:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you are looking at unfamiliar code and want understanding before making changes.
exampleUse: Paste a function, component, or query. The answer breaks it down into practical reasoning rather than textbook definitions.

title: Shape My Offer Into a Clear One-Page Pitch
description: Turns a vague offer into a sharper value proposition with target audience, promise, proof, and CTA.
prompt:
Help me turn my offer into a clear one-page pitch.
I will share what I sell, who it is for, the result it creates, and any proof I have.

Please create:
1. A simple positioning statement.
2. A customer-friendly description of the offer.
3. Key outcomes or benefits.
4. Common objections and how to answer them.
5. A simple call to action.

Offer details:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you have a service or product idea but your explanation still feels fuzzy.
exampleUse: Paste notes about what you sell and who it helps. The output becomes a stronger draft for a landing page, proposal, or sales message.

title: Prepare Better Responses to Customer Objections
description: Generates clear, calm responses to common objections like price, timing, fit, and trust.
prompt:
I want to prepare for customer objections.
I will share my offer and the objections I hear most often.

Please:
1. Group objections by theme.
2. Explain what each objection usually means underneath the surface.
3. Write a short, respectful response for each one.
4. Suggest proof, examples, or guarantees that could reduce the objection earlier.
5. Tell me which objections point to a positioning problem instead of a sales problem.

Context:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you want to handle objections more thoughtfully instead of sounding defensive or scripted.
exampleUse: Paste common buyer concerns and your offer details. The response helps you improve both your replies and your positioning.

title: Turn One Idea Into a Week of Short-Form Content
description: Repurposes one topic into multiple hooks, post angles, and formats for short-form content.
prompt:
I have one topic or idea and I want to turn it into multiple short-form posts.

Please create:
1. 7 post angles from different perspectives.
2. A strong hook for each.
3. A simple post structure for each one.
4. A mix of educational, opinion, and story-driven formats.
5. A note on which audience each angle will attract best.

My topic:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you have one strong idea but need more range and consistency from it.
exampleUse: Paste a lesson, insight, or story. The result gives you a week's worth of content directions without repeating the same post.

title: Turn a Raw Idea Into a Strong LinkedIn Post
description: Shapes a quick idea into a structured LinkedIn post with a hook, body, and thoughtful ending.
prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post from the idea I share.

Please:
1. Start with a hook that earns attention without sounding fake.
2. Expand the idea in a clear, readable way.
3. Keep the tone thoughtful and credible.
4. End with a simple closing line or question.
5. Give me one version that is shorter and one that is more reflective.

Idea:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you have a useful idea for LinkedIn but not the structure to make it land.
exampleUse: Paste a rough opinion or lesson from work. The reply turns it into a post that feels more deliberate and easier to publish.

title: Prepare for a Difficult Conversation Calmly
description: Helps you plan what to say in a tense conversation while staying honest, clear, and respectful.
prompt:
Help me prepare for a difficult conversation.
I will explain the relationship, what happened, what I need to say, and what I want the outcome to be.

Please:
1. Summarize the core issue clearly.
2. Suggest how to open the conversation calmly.
3. Help me express my point without escalating things.
4. Offer phrases for listening, boundaries, and next steps.
5. Warn me about wording that may make things worse.

Context:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when emotions are high and you want to go into a conversation with more clarity and less reactivity.
exampleUse: Describe the situation and what outcome you want. The response gives you better phrasing and helps you stay grounded.

title: Turn What I Have at Home Into a Simple Meal Plan
description: Generates practical meal ideas from the ingredients you already have, with less waste and less decision fatigue.
prompt:
I want a practical meal plan based on what I already have at home.
I will share the ingredients, dietary preferences, time available, and how many meals I need.

Please:
1. Suggest the best meals I can make.
2. Prioritize ingredients that should be used first.
3. Keep the meals simple and realistic.
4. Offer substitutions if something is missing.
5. End with a tiny shopping list only if truly necessary.

Ingredients and context:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you want an easier answer to “what should I cook?” without starting from scratch.
exampleUse: Paste a list of leftovers, pantry items, and your time limit. The response turns it into a low-stress meal plan.

title: Plan a Weekend Trip Around Budget, Vibe, and Time
description: Creates a short trip plan based on your budget, available time, interests, and energy level.
prompt:
Help me plan a weekend trip.
I will share where I am starting from, my budget, time available, who is going, and the kind of experience I want.

Please:
1. Suggest a trip style that fits the constraints.
2. Propose a simple itinerary.
3. Recommend how to split the budget.
4. List what to book first.
5. Point out any common planning mistakes for this kind of trip.

Trip context:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you want a quick, realistic trip plan without opening 30 browser tabs.
exampleUse: Paste your city, budget, and travel style. The result helps you shape a short getaway that actually fits your schedule.

title: Make a Smart Carry-On Packing List
description: Builds a compact packing list based on weather, trip type, laundry access, and baggage limits.
prompt:
Create a smart carry-on packing list for me.
I will share the destination, weather, trip length, activities, and airline limits.

Please:
1. Build a practical packing list.
2. Prioritize versatile clothing and essentials.
3. Flag items people often forget.
4. Suggest what not to bring.
5. Add a final pre-flight checklist.

Trip details:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when you want to pack lighter without forgetting the essentials.
exampleUse: Paste your destination, trip length, and weather. The answer gives you a more compact list with fewer unnecessary items.

title: Review My Budget and Show Me What Needs Attention
description: Looks at your income, expenses, and spending patterns to show what is healthy, risky, or worth adjusting.
prompt:
Help me review my monthly budget.
I will share my income, fixed costs, variable spending, savings goals, and any concerns.

Please:
1. Summarize the overall picture simply.
2. Highlight healthy patterns and problem areas.
3. Suggest 3 practical adjustments.
4. Tell me what to monitor next month.
5. Keep the advice realistic and non-judgmental.

Budget details:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this at the end of the month when you want a quick spending review and a calmer plan for next month.
exampleUse: Paste your numbers in rough form. The response turns them into a cleaner budget summary and a few practical next moves.

title: Find Easy Ways to Cut Subscription and Recurring Costs
description: Reviews recurring costs and spots which subscriptions to cancel, downgrade, share, or renegotiate.
prompt:
I want to cut recurring expenses without making life worse.
I will share my subscriptions, memberships, recurring charges, and what I actually use.

Please:
1. Group them into keep, review, downgrade, cancel, or replace.
2. Estimate which cuts would free up the most money.
3. Suggest lower-cost alternatives where relevant.
4. Tell me what to keep because it creates real value.
5. End with an easy action plan for this week.

My recurring costs:
[PASTE HERE]
whenToUse: Use this when your monthly expenses feel bloated and you want a practical cleanup list.
exampleUse: Paste your subscriptions and rough monthly costs. The reply helps you spot easy wins without having to analyze everything manually.
